title: A, an, the, or nothing?
lesson: English has two indefinite articles — 'a' before a consonant sound and 'an' before a vowel sound — plus the definite article 'the' for something specific. Many nouns take no article at all. The choice follows the sound a word starts with, not its spelling: 'an hour', 'a university'.

blanks.hint: follows the sound, not the letter

items.explanation: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound /ɒ/, so it takes 'an' — not 'a'.

items.text: I had {1} egg and {2} apple for breakfast.
items.explanation: Both 'egg' and 'apple' begin with a vowel sound, so each takes 'an'.

items.text: He drives {1} bus to work, plays {2} guitar in the evenings, and never watches {3} television.
items.explanation: A singular countable noun mentioned for the first time takes 'a' (a bus); musical instruments take 'the' (the guitar); 'watch television' is a fixed expression with no article.
Unique things — things that exist only once — take 'the' (the sun, the moon, the earth).

items.text: She bought {1} new coat. {2} coat is bright red.
items.explanation: The first mention of an unspecified noun takes 'a'; once the noun is known to the listener, the second mention takes 'the'.
